headlines.geopolitics: Far-left activists stay in 5-star Cuban hotel as island suffers total blackout
headlines.economy: A Window Into the Lives of New York City Renters
headlines.domestic: The Billionaire Funding France's Far Right

metadata.daily_do: Check your hotel accommodations if you're planning any solidarity trips — apparently your choice of lodging sends a message

clips.caption: When your solidarity trip includes room service 💀
Duke destroys luxury activism in 30 seconds
The most expensive protest in history

script.text: Look, I've been to Afghanistan twice and I never stayed anywhere half as nice as these people's solidarity hotels.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. It's Sunday, March 22nd, and we have three stories that'll make you question everything about modern activism.

First up, geopolitics. Far-left activists including Representative Ilhan Omar's daughter and Twitch streamer Hasan Piker stayed at five-star Cuban hotels while protesting Trump-era sanctions, drawing criticism as the island suffered total blackouts.
Wait, five-star hotels?

The CodePink-sponsored delegation claimed U.S. law required luxury accommodations, though fact-checkers disputed this. Secretary of State Marco Rubio called for regime change while Pentagon officials denied invasion plans.
Oh this is gonna be good.
Economy. The New York Times Magazine featured photographer Gail Albert Halaban's project documenting NYC renters in their apartments, shot from across the street with permission.
From five-star hotels to studio apartments. The contrast is, uh, noticeable.

And domestic. French billionaire Pierre-Édouard Stérin is financing far-right political projects aimed at making France quote less Muslim, more Catholic and more capitalist. His program claims to have trained thousands of candidates running for municipal office.
Ah, the old playbook.
So we've got luxury revolutionaries, apartment voyeurism, and a French billionaire trying to make France great again. This is why I love Sundays.
Let's dive into Cuba. Bree, I'm gonna start with you because I know you have thoughts about economic sanctions.
Look, I think the sanctions discussion is valid, but can we talk about the optics here? You're protesting poverty while staying at the Gran Hotel Bristol?
Thank you. This is exactly what drives me insane about—
Wait, let me finish. The humanitarian crisis in Cuba is real. People are literally sitting in the dark while these activists are posting Instagram stories from hotel pools.
I looked up this hotel. Room service menu has lobster thermidor. For a solidarity trip.
Duke, your take?
This is performative activism at its worst. You want to help Cubans? Send money. Send medicine. Don't fly down there for a luxury vacation and call it solidarity.
But Duke, they did bring aid. CodePink says they delivered thousands of pounds of supplies.
Oh, so the five-star hotels were just a coincidence? Come on.
Hasan Piker tried to claim U.S. law required them to stay at five-star hotels. That got fact-checked so fast it gave me whiplash.
Right, the community note said U.S. law only prevents Americans from staying at venues owned by the Cuban government. Not exactly the same thing.
Look, I'm not defending the hotel choice, but let's not lose sight of the bigger issue. These sanctions have been in place for decades and Cuba is still—
Still a communist dictatorship? Yeah, that's the point.
But the people suffering aren't the government officials. It's regular Cubans who can't get basic necessities.
And lifting sanctions helps the regime more than it helps the people. We've seen this playbook before.
What's interesting is the timing. This delegation arrives the same weekend Cuba announces a total blackout. The whole island, no electricity.
So Cuban families are sitting in the dark while Twitch streamers are live-tweeting from air-conditioned hotel lobbies. The irony is so thick you could cut it with a machete.
Okay, that's actually a fair point about the timing.
My unit did humanitarian work in Afghanistan. You know where we stayed? Tents. Sometimes not even tents.
That's different though. You were deployed military—
Is it? Because we were there to help people too. And somehow we managed to do it without room service.
I'm picturing Hasan Piker explaining to a Cuban family sitting in the dark that his solidarity required a spa day.
Stop, stop.
What worries me is Marco Rubio's response. He's talking about regime change, Pentagon officials are denying invasion plans. This kind of activist tourism just gives hawks more ammunition.
Exactly. You want to help Cuban people? Don't hand the hardliners a propaganda gift.
And that's what frustrates me. There are legitimate questions about sanctions policy, but this luxury activism undermines the whole conversation.
The most expensive protest in history and it backfired. That's gotta be some kind of record.
Professor, this reminds me of something you've talked about before. Privileged activism during times of crisis.
Indeed. This takes me back to 1968, the height of anti-Vietnam War protests. You had wealthy college students flying to Paris to meet with North Vietnamese officials.
Jane Fonda's trip to Hanoi is the most famous example, but she wasn't alone. There was a whole cottage industry of what we might call solidarity tourism.
Wait, Jane Fonda went to Vietnam?
Oh yes. 1972. She posed with North Vietnamese anti-aircraft guns, visited POW camps, made radio broadcasts. The backlash was immediate and lasted decades.
Yeah, they called her Hanoi Jane. My dad still won't watch her movies.

The parallel is striking. Wealthy Americans traveling to enemy territory during wartime, claiming to represent the oppressed while enjoying privileges unavailable to the very people they claim to support.
But Doc, wasn't the anti-war movement ultimately vindicated by history?
The movement's goals perhaps, but the methods often backfired. Fonda later apologized for the Hanoi photos. She realized they hurt the cause more than they helped.
The lesson from 1968 is that privilege and protest make uneasy bedfellows. When your activism looks like a vacation, you've already lost the moral argument.
So basically, if you're staying somewhere nicer than the people you're trying to help, you're doing it wrong?
That's a rather crude way to put it, but yes. Authenticity matters in activism. Always has.
The difference is Fonda was protesting a war. These people are protesting... what exactly? Economic sanctions while the Cuban government keeps its people in the dark?
The irony is delicious, isn't it? They're protesting American policy while enjoying American-level luxury that regular Cubans will never see.
And like Fonda, they may find that the photos from this trip follow them longer than they expect.
Alright, time for Rapid Fire because apparently the world decided to get weird this week. First up, AI-generated sexualized videos were removed from TikTok after a BBC investigation.
Oh no.
Yeah, it's as bad as it sounds. Deepfake technology making fake intimate videos that look real. TikTok's playing whack-a-mole trying to remove them.
This is why I don't let my kids on social media unsupervised.
The legal implications here are staggering. We're talking about non-consensual intimate imagery that looks completely real.

Moving on to Australia, where officials are urging people to work from home and drive slower to save fuel. Which, I mean, it's Australia. Everything there is trying to kill you anyway, might as well add expensive gas.
Drive slower to save fuel. Revolutionary thinking.
Actually, that's not terrible policy during an energy crunch.
Next story is gonna give you nightmares. Barbies sold at a Missouri store were potentially contaminated with fentanyl.
What?
How does that even happen?
Nobody knows yet. But imagine explaining to your kid why Barbie needs to go to the police station for testing.
In my day, the most dangerous thing about toys was choking hazards.
And finally, TSA airport lines are sparking rage as ICE deployment plans get revealed. Apparently nobody told travelers that increased immigration enforcement means longer security lines.
Security costs time. This isn't rocket science.
But the communication around it has been terrible. People are missing flights because nobody explained the new procedures.
I love that we live in a world where AI deepfakes, fentanyl Barbies, and solidarity hotels all exist simultaneously. What a time to be alive.
That's one way to put it.
Alright, time for today's Daily Do. Given everything we've talked about, here's what you can actually do with this information.
If you're planning any kind of solidarity trip, activism travel, or humanitarian work, think about your accommodations. Your hotel choice sends a message about your priorities.
You don't have to sleep on the ground, but maybe don't livestream from a five-star pool while talking about economic justice. The optics matter, and they can undermine your entire message.
Or just send the money instead of taking the trip. Probably more effective anyway.
That's actually not wrong. Direct aid often works better than awareness campaigns.
Plus you avoid the whole luxury activism controversy. Win-win.
As Peggy used to say, if you want to help people, help them. Don't help yourself to a vacation and call it charity.
Words to live by. That's The Friction for today. I'm Leo Barros.
Stay curious, stay skeptical, and maybe check your hotel reviews before you book that solidarity trip. We'll see you tomorrow.
